evidences.content: #### Supply & demand constraints for tantalum Tantalum, along with tin, tungsten, and gold, are all members of the 3TG group, which are most popularly referred to as “conflict minerals” given their relevance and contribution to ongoing conflicts in East Africa. At the turn of the millennium, Australia and Brazil were leading producers of tantalum, but growing extraction costs depressed margins for Australian and Brazilian tantalum mines. Furthermore, the commodities boom of the early 2000s compelled most large-scale mining (LSM) firms to focus on more lucrative and mature commodity markets. To fill this supply gap, East African nations like Rwanda, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Burundi seized market share, accounting for well over half (57%) of worldwide tantalum production. [...] Tantalum’s usefulness for both consumer and military applications has led to its classification as a Technology-Critical Element (TCE). Artisanal mining, particularly in countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo, remains a significant contributor, though it is associated with issues of ethical sourcing and conflict minerals. Placer deposits, formed by the erosion and transport of tantalum-bearing pegmatites, have historically accounted for about 40% of global tantalum production, but today they account for about 20%.
